name: Nevada
tooltip: Nevada — Cat 2: NRS 597.770 plus 2025 deepfake amendments. Registration required to enforce post-mortem.
rightOfPublicity: Yes — NRS 597.770 to 597.810. Covers commercial use of name, voice, signature, photograph, or likeness. Living and deceased persons.
postMortem: Yes — 50 years after death. NRS 597.790(1). **Important quirk: enforcement of post-mortem rights requires registration with the Nevada Secretary of State.** Without registration, the post-mortem claim is procedurally barred even if the underlying violation is clear.
aiCoverage: Limited but expanding. NRS 200.780 (NCII, with 2025 SB 213 / SB 263 amendments expanding to synthetic content). AB 73 (2025) covers election synthetic media, signed by Governor Lombardo with a January 1, 2026 effective date.
remedy: Civil suit under NRS 597.810 for actual damages plus profits, with $750 statutory floor and attorney's fees. **For post-mortem claims, register with the Secretary of State first** — failing to do this is the most common procedural mistake.
whatsInteresting: Nevada's registration requirement is the trap. The statute looks strong on paper — 50-year post-mortem, $750 statutory floor — but if a deceased performer's estate hasn't registered the right with the Secretary of State, courts will dismiss the claim. If you're managing a Nevada-domiciled estate (which includes a lot of Las Vegas residency entertainers), registration is the first step before any other estate planning.
